summary: Fowler argues that effective digital platforms must be self-service, compelling internal products that reduce 'backlog coupling' - the coordination overhead that occurs when delivery teams depend on centralized operations teams. He contrasts a failed 'superficial private cloud' approach (where self-service was limited to provisioning locked-down VMs while everything else required tickets) with successful autonomous approaches. However, he notes that full autonomy creates technology diversification drag, requiring teams to research and maintain their own infrastructure choices. The solution is treating platforms as compelling internal products that teams choose to use rather than mandated standards.
key_points: Backlog coupling - dependencies across team work queues - makes tasks 10-12x slower and damages team accountability and motivation
Self-service must extend beyond provisioning to include configuration, management, and operation of platform capabilities
Full team autonomy increases innovation and engagement but creates technology diversification drag as teams must research and maintain infrastructure choices
Platforms must be compelling products that are easier to consume than building your own thing - not mandated monopolies
Platform teams and application teams both follow 'you build it, you run it' but with clearly separated responsibilities at the API boundary
money_quotes: A digital platform is a foundation of self-service APIs, tools, services, knowledge and support which are arranged as a compelling internal product.
We notice that when change is hard and slow, then any failures in the change process cause further delays.
The tasks that had to wait for another team were 10-12x slower in elapsed time.
This explicitly trades off an increase in alignment for overall reduced autonomy, responsiveness and ability to respond to change. This cannot be the only way.
A key ingredient for success in finding this balance is that platforms must be compelling to use, they cannot stand on a mandate alone.
Netflix describes their centralised tooling as 'the paved road' - teams do not have to make use of the tooling but are responsible for all the costs of maintaining their own alternatives.
The platform team ideally doesn't even know what applications are running on the platform, they are only responsible for the availability of the platform services themselves.
key_insight: Effective platforms reduce backlog coupling through genuine self-service while remaining compelling enough that teams choose to use them rather than building alternatives.
